summary: O'Neil reflects on Halloween's social significance as a celebration of generosity and neighborliness, where normal social rules are suspended. Her nostalgic appreciation is disrupted when she encounters a Slate advice column where someone complains about poor children trick-or-treating in their wealthy neighborhood. She argues that Halloween represents a brief alternative economic system based on actual sharing, and that the complaint reveals how some people feel threatened by this rule-breaking and want to impose class-based hierarchies even on this egalitarian tradition.
key_points: Halloween's true value lies not in candy or costumes but in the suspension of social rules and celebration of generosity
The holiday represents a brief alternative economic system of genuine sharing, distinct from the so-called 'sharing economy'
Class anxiety can distort even innocent traditions, turning moments of communal joy into opportunities for exclusion
Those who examine and fret about their place in social hierarchies may feel threatened by rule-breaking celebrations
The complaint about poor kids trick-or-treating reveals it was never about the cost of candy but about maintaining class boundaries
money_quotes: Costumes are the excuse to tell each other how amazing they look, and candy is the excuse to symbolically exchange a token of friendship.
I pretty much had kids in part so I could start going trick-or-treating again, that's how much I love it.
It was addictive and magical nonetheless because of the human interactions and the broken rules. Thrilling.
I'd never seen a whiff of class warfare in Halloween until this ridiculous question.
Halloween represents, as an alternative – if very brief – economic system in which we all actually share (versus the so-called 'sharing economy').

Instead of reveling in the inherent rule-breaking nature of Halloween, in other words, this person is threatened by it and wants to control it and make it conform to the class-based system they are familiar with.
Obviously it's not really about how much Halloween candy costs.
key_insight: Halloween's brief suspension of social hierarchies through unconditional sharing threatens those who are invested in maintaining class distinctions.
